subject: ap-chemistry
title: Thermochemistry & Calorimetry
stimulus:
A student performs an experiment to determine the enthalpy of combustion of 2-propanol, C H OH(l), which
combusts in oxygen according to the equation above. The student heats a sample of water by burning some of
the C
H
OH(l) that is in an alcohol burner, as represented below. The alcohol burner uses a wick to draw liquid
up into the flame. The mass of C
H
OH(l) combusted is determined by weighing the alcohol burner before and
after combustion.
Data from the experiment are given in the table below.
Mass of C
H
OH(l) combusted 0.55 g
Mass of water heated 125.00 g
Initial temperature of water 22.0°C
Final temperature of water 51.1°C
Specific heat of water 4.18 J/(g·°C)

parts.prompt:
Calculate the magnitude of the heat energy, in kJ, absorbed by the water. (Assume that the energy released
from the combustion is completely transferred to the water.)

scoring_points.description: For the correct heat absorbed by the water using $q = mc\Delta T$: 1 point
wrong_examples: Using mass of 2-propanol (0.55 g) instead of mass of water (125.00 g) in $q = mc\Delta T$.
Using the specific heat of 2-propanol instead of water — use the specific heat of water (4.18 J/(g·°C)).
common_traps: $q = mc\Delta T$ uses the mass and specific heat of the substance being heated (the water), not the fuel.

parts.prompt:
Based on the experimental data, if one mole of C
H
OH(l) is combusted, how much heat, in kJ, is released?
Report your answer with the correct number of significant figures.
parts.scoring_notes: Molar mass of 2-propanol (C$_3$H$_7$OH = C$_3$H$_8$O) = 60.09 g/mol. Moles burned = 0.55 g / 60.09 g/mol = 9.15 × 10$^{-3}$ mol. Heat per mole = 15.2 kJ / 9.15 × 10$^{-3}$ mol ≈ 1,660 kJ/mol. With 2 sig figs (limited by 0.55 g mass): $\Delta H_{\text{comb}} \approx -1700\text{ kJ/mol}$ (sign convention: exothermic, heat released).

scoring_points.description: For the correct molar enthalpy of combustion with appropriate significant figures: 1 point

required_elements: Heat per mole = 15.2 kJ / 9.2 × 10$^{-3}$ mol ≈ 1700 kJ/mol (2 sig figs).
alternatives.correct_example:
$n(\text{C}_3\text{H}_7\text{OH}) = \frac{0.55\text{ g}}{60.09\text{ g/mol}} = 9.15 \times 10^{-3}\text{ mol}$
Heat per mole $= \frac{15.2\text{ kJ}}{9.15 \times 10^{-3}\text{ mol}} \approx 1660\text{ kJ/mol}$
Reported to 2 significant figures: $1700\text{ kJ/mol}$ released (or $-1700\text{ kJ/mol}$ for $\Delta H$).

wrong_examples: Dividing by the mass in grams (0.55) directly — must convert grams to moles using molar mass.
Reporting answer to 4 sig figs — the mass (0.55 g) limits the answer to 2 sig figs.
common_traps: Significant figures: the least precise measurement is the mass of alcohol (0.55 g, 2 sig figs). The final answer must be rounded to 2 sig figs.

parts.prompt:
A second student performs the experiment using the same mass of water at the same initial temperature.
However, the student uses an alcohol burner containing C
H
OH(l) that is contaminated with water, which
is miscible with C
H
OH(l). The difference in mass of the alcohol burner before and after the combustion in
this experiment is also 0.55 g. Would the final temperature of the water in the beaker heated by the
alcohol burner in this experiment be greater than, less than, or equal to the final temperature of the water
in the beaker in the first student's experiment? Justify your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: Less than. The 0.55 g mass loss includes both 2-propanol and water. Since water does not combust, less than 0.55 g of 2-propanol is actually burned. Less fuel → less heat released → smaller temperature increase of the water in the beaker.

scoring_points.description: For 'less than' with the correct reasoning about the fuel being diluted with non-combustible water: 1 point

required_elements: The 0.55 g mass change includes evaporated water from the contaminated fuel. Only the 2-propanol component combusts; water does not contribute to heat release. Less actual 2-propanol burned → less heat → lower final temperature.
alternatives.correct_example: Less than. The 0.55 g mass loss of the contaminated burner includes both 2-propanol and water. Since water does not combust and releases no heat, the amount of 2-propanol actually burned is less than 0.55 g. Less fuel combusted → less heat released → smaller temperature increase → lower final temperature of the water in the beaker.

wrong_examples: Greater than — less fuel is burned, releasing less heat, so the temperature rise is smaller, not larger.
Equal to — water in the fuel does not combust; the actual mass of 2-propanol burned is less than 0.55 g.
common_traps: The mass change of the burner = mass of fuel evaporated/combusted + mass of water evaporated. If the fuel is diluted, less actual combustible material is present in the 0.55 g.

parts.prompt: The accepted value for the molar enthalpy of combustion of 2-propanol is −2006 kJ/mol. Calculate the percent error for the student's experimental value determined in part (b).
parts.scoring_notes: Using experimental value ≈ 1660 kJ/mol (or 1700 kJ/mol depending on sig figs): % error = |experimental − accepted| / accepted × 100% = |1660 − 2006| / 2006 × 100% = 346/2006 × 100% ≈ 17%. Accept answers in the range 15–20% depending on intermediate rounding.

scoring_points.description: For the correct percent error calculation with the accepted value: 1 point

required_elements: Consistent with the experimental value from part (b); answer ≈ 15–20%.
alternatives.correct_example:
$\%\text{ error} = \frac{|1660 - 2006|}{2006} \times 100\% = \frac{346}{2006} \times 100\% \approx 17\%$
(Accept 'consistent with' answers if part (b) answer differs slightly due to rounding.)

wrong_examples: Dividing by the experimental value instead of the accepted value — percent error uses the accepted value in the denominator.
Not taking the absolute value — percent error is always reported as a positive quantity.
common_traps: % error = |experimental − accepted| / accepted × 100%. Always divide by the accepted (true) value, not the experimental value.
